name: max_serialized_size_fits_in_u32
reason: The max serialized size (0x100000 = 1 MiB) fits in a u32, meaning the spec silently constrains hash maps to at most 1 MiB serialized — this may be too small for large hash maps and the limit is buried in an opaque constant with no documentation of why this bound was chosen

name: max_serialized_size_is_power_of_two
reason: The constant is exactly 2^20 — a suspiciously round number that may be an arbitrary placeholder rather than a carefully derived bound, and downstream code relying on this specific value could break if the real protocol needs a different limit

name: max_size_smaller_than_usize_max
reason: The max serialized size is far below `usize::MAX / 2` — any code that adds the max size to another offset will never overflow, but this means the spec artificially limits hash map sizes to a tiny fraction of addressable memory without justification

name: exec_reveals_opaque_constant
reason: The opaque spec can be revealed by any caller to learn the exact constant — the `opaque` annotation provides no real encapsulation since `ckeyhashmap_max_serialized_size_exec` already leaks the value through its ensures clause and any proof can call `reveal`

name: two_max_sizes_fit_in_usize
reason: Two maximally-sized serialized hash maps can be summed without overflow — this could mask buffer overflow bugs in code that concatenates two serialized hash maps, since the spec allows it but the bound may be too generous or too restrictive depending on the actual protocol requirements
